perturbation.original_text: If Disclosing Party gives its written consent to Recipient to disclose Information or Items to a third party, Recipient shall require the third party to execute a non-disclosure agreement in form, and substance satisfactory to Disclosing Party. Recipient shall promptly furnish Disclosing Party with a copy of all such non-disclosure agreements.
perturbation.changed_text: If Disclosing Party gives its written consent to Recipient to disclose Information or Items to a third party, Recipient should encourage the third party to execute a non-disclosure agreement. Recipient shall furnish Disclosing Party with a copy of any such non-disclosure agreements if available.
perturbation.explanation: By changing 'shall require' to 'should encourage' and 'shall promptly furnish' to 'shall furnish...if available,' the obligation to ensure a third party signs an NDA and to provide a copy to the disclosing party becomes discretionary. This weakens the protection of confidential information.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York Uniform Trade Secrets Act (NYUTSA)
perturbation.law_explanation: Under the New York Uniform Trade Secrets Act (NYUTSA), specifically N.Y. C.P.L.R. § 4548, reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy are important. By weakening the requirement for the Recipient to ensure third parties sign NDAs and to promptly provide copies, the modified text undermines the 'reasonable efforts' necessary to protect trade secrets. The original clause provides a verifiable procedure for maintaining confidentiality. The changed text opens the door for a defense against misappropriation claims by claiming that reasonable efforts were not taken to protect the information.

Privileged communications; electronic communication thereof. No communication privileged under this article shall lose its privileged character for the sole reason that it is communicated by electronic means or because persons necessary for the delivery or facilitation of such electronic communication may have access to the content of the communication.
perturbation.justification: The changed text weakens the original obligation to protect confidential information, creating a contradiction. The original text mandated the recipient to 'require' third parties to sign NDAs, and 'shall promptly furnish' copies. The modified text changes this to 'should encourage' and 'shall furnish...if available,' making the NDA requirement discretionary and the provision of copies conditional. The Law Explanation references the New York Uniform Trade Secrets Act (NYUTSA), specifically N.Y. C.P.L.R. § 4548, and reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing N.Y. C.P.L.R. § 4548, discusses privileged communications. The weakening of the NDA requirement undermines the 'reasonable efforts' necessary to protect trade secrets, as claimed by the Law Explanation.

perturbation.original_text: You agree that you will only discuss the Matter with Mr. Al Weber, Chief Executive Officer, or Mr. Thomas V. Gilboy, Chief Financial Officer of the Disclosing Party. Under no circumstances will you discuss the Matter, or admit that the Matter has been a topic of discussion with any other employees of the Disclosing Party, or any other third party. In addition, during 2000, you agree not to undertake any unusual efforts to research the business, operations, prospects or finances of the Disclosing Party, where such research efforts would include discussions with any third party, except in the ordinary course of business.
perturbation.changed_text: You agree that you will primarily discuss the Matter with Mr. Al Weber, Chief Executive Officer, or Mr. Thomas V. Gilboy, Chief Financial Officer of the Disclosing Party. You will try to avoid discussing the Matter with other employees of the Disclosing Party, or any other third party. In addition, during 2000, you agree to refrain from undertaking any extraordinary efforts to research the business, operations, prospects or finances of the Disclosing Party, where such research efforts would include discussions with any third party, except in the normal course of business.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'will only discuss' to 'will primarily discuss,' 'under no circumstances will you discuss' to 'will try to avoid discussing,' and 'unusual efforts' to 'extraordinary efforts' introduces ambiguity and weakens the restriction on discussing the matter with anyone other than the specified individuals. The replaced words make the original obligations non-compulsory.
perturbation.law_explanation: By weakening the obligation to keep discussions strictly limited to specific individuals, the modified text could potentially lead to broader dissemination of non-public information about the company. This increased risk of information leakage could facilitate insider trading, violating SEC Rule 10b-5, which prohibits the use of manipulative or deceptive devices in connection with the purchase or sale of securities. The original text sought to reduce this risk by imposing strict confidentiality, whereas the modified text undermines this protection.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 17 CFR § 240.10b-5 - Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 17 CFR § 240.10b-5 - Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. CFR prev | next § 240.10b-5 Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. It shall be unlawful for any person, directly or indirectly, by the use of any means or instrumentality of interstate commerce, or of the mails or of any facility of any national securities exchange , (a) To employ any device, scheme, or artifice to defraud, (b) To make any untrue statement of a material fact or to omit to state a material fact necessary in order to make the statements made, in the light of the circumstances under which they were made, not misleading, or (c) To engage in any act, practice, or course of business which operates or would operate as a fraud or deceit upon any person, in connection with the purchase or sale of any security . (Sec. 10; 48 Stat. 891 ; 15 U.S.C. 78j ) [ 13 FR 8183 , Dec. 22, 1948, as amended at 16 FR 7928 , Aug. 11, 1951] CFR Toolbox Law about... Articles from Wex Table of Popular Names Parallel Table of Authorities
perturbation.justification: The changed text weakens the original confidentiality obligations by using terms like 'primarily discuss' and 'try to avoid discussing,' making the restrictions non-compulsory. This increases the risk of disseminating non-public information, which could potentially lead to insider trading, a violation of SEC Rule 10b-5. The snippet from 17 CFR § 240.10b-5 prohibits 'any device, scheme, or artifice to defraud' and 'any act, practice, or course of business which operates or would operate as a fraud or deceit upon any person, in connection with the purchase or sale of any security.' By weakening the restrictions, the modified contract facilitates a scenario where insider information could be used for fraudulent activities, directly contradicting the rule.
